title: Daniel 7
subtitle: The Four Beasts and the Son of Man

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Vision of Four Beasts

sections.header: Verses 1–14 — “One Like a Son of Man Coming with the Clouds of Heaven”

heb.paragraph: "One like a son of man (bar ʾĕnāsh), coming with the clouds of heaven. He was given authority, glory and sovereign power; all nations worshipped him. His dominion is everlasting" (vv.13–14). Four beasts from the sea: lion (Babylon), bear (Medo-Persia), leopard (Greece), terrifying fourth beast with iron teeth and ten horns (Rome). Ancient of Days on his fire-throne, books opened. The son of man receives eternal dominion. Jesus uses "Son of Man" over 80 times in the Gospels — all trace to this verse.

refs.note: Jesus before the Sanhedrin: "You will see the Son of Man sitting at the right hand of the Mighty One and coming on the clouds of heaven" — the moment they accuse him of blasphemy. He claims to be Daniel’s figure.
Look, he is coming with the clouds, and every eye will see him — Revelation’s opening vision quotes Daniel 7:13.
The throne room of heaven — directly developed from Daniel 7:9–10.
All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me — Jesus claims the authority Dan 7:14 assigns to the son of man.

notes.note: Four beasts from the sea. The lion with eagle wings: Babylon. Wings torn off, stands like a man — possibly Nebuchadnezzar’s humbling and restoration.
The fourth beast: terrifying, iron teeth, ten horns. A little horn with human eyes and a boastful mouth — the primary OT source for NT antichrist theology.
The Ancient of Days: clothing white as snow, hair like pure wool, throne of fire. Thousands upon thousands attend him. The heavenly courtroom.
The son of man: divine authority, eternal kingdom, universal worship. Jesus claims this at his trial (Mark 14:62). This is the title he uses most.

Calvin reads the Ancient of Days as a vision of God’s eternal kingship revealed in time. The white clothing symbolises purity; the fire symbolises both judgment and purification. The opened books are the records of all human deeds.

Calvin identifies the son of man as Christ without hesitation: no merely human figure could receive "all nations worshipping him" and "an everlasting dominion." The cloud-riding is a divine prerogative (Ps 104:3).

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes that "Ancient of Days" (ʿattiq yōmin) is unique to Daniel. The white hair denotes wisdom and venerability, not weakness. The fire-throne parallels Ezekiel 1’s merkabah vision.

The NET observes that "one like a son of man" uses the Aramaic preposition kə ("like"), suggesting the figure resembles a human but may not be merely human. The cloud-riding is elsewhere exclusively a divine activity (Ps 68:4; Isa 19:1).

longman.source: Tremper Longman III, Daniel, NIVAC (1999) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Longman: the Ancient of Days scene is the most developed vision of God as judge in the OT. The fire is both purifying and consuming. The books are the permanent record. Nothing is hidden from this court.

Longman: whether the son of man is an individual or collective figure in the original context, Jesus’ appropriation of the title settles the question for Christian readers. He claims it; the NT develops it; the church confesses it.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Daniel, WBC (1989) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay reads the scene as a two-phase transfer of power: first, the beast’s authority is removed (vv.11–12); second, the son of man’s authority is granted (vv.13–14). The gap between the two phases is the space in which the reader lives.

Goldingay: the cloud-riding is the decisive clue. In every other OT occurrence, cloud-riding is God’s prerogative. The figure "like a son of man" approaches God’s throne and receives God’s authority. The text pushes the reader toward a more-than-human identification.

collins.source: John J. Collins, Daniel, Hermeneia (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Collins provides the standard critical identification: lion = Babylon, bear = Media, leopard = Persia, fourth beast = Greece (with the little horn = Antiochus IV). The traditional identification (fourth = Rome) became dominant through Christian interpretation but requires combining Media and Persia into one kingdom.

Collins argues the "son of man" originally represented the angelic figure Michael (cf. 10:13, 21; 12:1) or a collective symbol for "the saints of the Most High" (v.27). The individual-messianic reading developed in the first century and is reflected in the Similitudes of Enoch and the Gospels.

hist.context: Chapter 7 is the structural pivot of the book: the last chapter in Aramaic, the first apocalyptic vision, and the mirror of ch.2’s statue (same four kingdoms, different imagery). The "sea" from which the beasts emerge is the primordial chaos-ocean of ancient Near Eastern mythology (cf. Gen 1:2). The "Ancient of Days" is the oldest representation of God as an aged, white-haired figure in Jewish literature. The "son of man" figure is the most debated identity in the OT: individual Messiah, angelic being, or corporate symbol for the faithful remnant.

sections.header: Verses 15–28 — “The Saints Will Receive the Kingdom Forever”

heb.paragraph: "The saints (qaddishē) of the Most High will receive the kingdom and will possess it forever" (v.18). Daniel asks for the interpretation. The four beasts are four kingdoms (v.17). The little horn wages war against the saints and defeats them — until the Ancient of Days pronounces judgment in their favour (vv.21–22). The horn will oppress the saints for "a time, times and half a time" (v.25) — 3.5 years, the template for Revelation’s tribulation period.

refs.note: The holy city trampled for 42 months; two witnesses prophesy for 1,260 days — developing Daniel’s 3.5-year period.
The beast given authority for 42 months, to wage war against God’s holy people — directly from Daniel 7:21, 25.
The saints will judge the world; they will judge angels — the Pauline version of Daniel 7:22, 27.
Come, take your inheritance, the kingdom prepared for you — the saints receiving the kingdom.

notes.note: "Four beasts = four kingdoms. But the saints will receive the kingdom." Beasts temporary; saints’ kingdom forever.
The horn defeats the saints UNTIL God intervenes. Faithfulness does not guarantee immediate victory; it guarantees ultimate vindication.
"A time, times and half a time." 3.5 years: the template for Revelation’s tribulation. A limit is set on suffering.
Sovereignty and power handed to the holy people. The end: not destruction but transfer of power. The saints inherit.

Calvin: the saints lose UNTIL God acts. This is the hardest aspect of Daniel’s vision — the righteous are genuinely defeated before they are vindicated. Calvin counsels patience: the "until" has an expiration date.

Calvin reads the kingdom-transfer as the progressive triumph of the gospel: not a single political event but the gradual extension of Christ’s reign through the church, completed at the second advent.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes that "time, times and half a time" = 1 + 2 + 0.5 = 3.5 years. The expression became a fixed apocalyptic formula for the period of eschatological tribulation, adopted directly by Revelation.

The NET observes the parallel between v.14 (the son of man’s kingdom) and v.27 (the saints’ kingdom). The saints receive what the son of man receives, suggesting either identification or representation.

longman.source: Tremper Longman III, Daniel, NIVAC (1999) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Longman: the phrase "defeating them — until" is the most pastoral word in Daniel 7. Persecution is real; defeat is genuine; but the word "until" limits it. The suffering has a terminus.

Longman: the vision ends with the saints possessing the kingdom "forever." The last word of the Aramaic section (chs.2–7) is eternal sovereignty for God’s people. Everything between now and then is penultimate.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Daniel, WBC (1989) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: the 3.5 years is half of seven — the completeness-number cut in half. The tribulation is deliberately incomplete, a broken period that signals it will end before reaching its natural conclusion.

Goldingay notes that Daniel is "deeply troubled" at the end. The visionary who receives the most exalted prophecy is the most personally disturbed. Apocalyptic knowledge does not produce serenity; it produces holy dread.

collins.source: John J. Collins, Daniel, Hermeneia (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Collins identifies the "saints of the Most High" (qaddishē ʿelyōnîn) as potentially angelic beings (as "holy ones" often means in the OT, e.g., Deut 33:2–3; Ps 89:5) or as the faithful human community, or both. The ambiguity may be deliberate: heavenly and earthly faithful share the same kingdom.
Collins reads the 3.5-year period as alluding to the desecration of the temple under Antiochus IV (167–164 BC), which lasted approximately three and a half years according to 1 Maccabees.

hist.context: The "time, times and half a time" (3.5 years = 42 months = 1,260 days) becomes the most important time-measurement in apocalyptic literature. Revelation uses it in 11:2, 11:3, 12:6, 12:14, 13:5. The period likely originates from the approximate duration of Antiochus IV’s temple desecration (167–164 BC). The saints’ kingdom (v.27) is described in the same terms as the son of man’s (v.14), suggesting identification between the two. The chapter ends with Daniel deeply troubled (v.28) — the apocalyptic visionary is not triumphant but devastated.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV\u201cAfter that, I looked, and there before me was another beast, one that looked like a leopard. And on its back it had four wings like those of a bird. This beast had four heads, and it was given authority to rule.ESVAfter this I looked, and behold, another, like a leopard, with four wings of a bird on its back. And the beast had four heads, and dominion was given to it.NIV\u201cAnd there before me was a second beast, which looked like a bear. It was raised up on one of its sides, and it had three ribs in its mouth between its teeth. It was told, \u2018Get up and eat your fill of flesh!\u2019ESVAnd behold, another beast, a second one, like a bear. It was raised up on one side. It had three ribs in its mouth between its teeth; and it was told, \u2018Arise, devour much flesh.\u2019

src.title: The Prayer of Nabonidus (4Q242, Dead Sea Scrolls)
src.quote: A fragmentary Qumran text describes a Babylonian king struck with illness for seven years who is healed by a Jewish exorcist — strikingly parallel to Nebuchadnezzar’s madness in Daniel 4.
src.note: The Prayer of Nabonidus may preserve an older version of the tradition behind Daniel 4, or reflect a common Near Eastern motif of royal humbling. Either way, it confirms the antiquity of the Daniel traditions.

src.title: 1 Maccabees 1–2 (2nd c. BC)
src.quote: Describes the historical desecration of the Jerusalem temple by Antiochus IV Epiphanes in 167 BC — the event Daniel 8:9–14 and 11:31 prophesy as the “abomination of desolation.”
src.note: The fulfilment is documented in non-biblical Jewish sources, confirming Daniel’s prophetic precision whether the prophecy is dated to the 6th or 2nd century BC.

src.title: Josephus, Antiquities 10.11 (1st c. AD)
src.quote: Josephus records that when Alexander the Great approached Jerusalem (332 BC), the high priest showed him Daniel’s prophecy about a Greek king conquering Persia, and Alexander was so impressed he offered sacrifices at the temple.
src.note: Whether historically accurate or legendary, Josephus’s account shows that Daniel’s prophecies were understood as referring to Greek conquest well before the Christian era.

rows.label: The Interpretation — 7:15–28
rows.text: Four kingdoms. Horn oppresses 3.5 years. "The SAINTS will receive the kingdom FOREVER."

hebtext.note: "One like a son of man (bar ʾĕnāsh), coming with the clouds of heaven. He was given authority, glory and sovereign power; all nations worshipped him. His dominion is everlasting" (vv.13–14). Four beasts from the sea: lion (Babylon), bear (Medo-Persia), leopard (Greece), terrifying fourth beast with iron teeth and ten horns (Rome). Ancient of Days on his fire-throne, books opened. The son of man receives eternal dominion. Jesus uses "Son of Man" over 80 times in the Gospels — all trace to this verse.

"The saints (qaddishē) of the Most High will receive the kingdom and will possess it forever" (v.18). Daniel asks for the interpretation. The four beasts are four kingdoms (v.17). The little horn wages war against the saints and defeats them — until the Ancient of Days pronounces judgment in their favour (vv.21–22). The horn will oppress the saints for "a time, times and half a time" (v.25) — 3.5 years, the template for Revelation’s tribulation period.

themes.note: Sovereignty AND Prophecy peak at 10: the Son of Man vision is the most christologically dense passage in the OT. 3.5 years becomes Revelation’s tribulation template.

coaching.tip: 'One like a son of man, coming with the clouds of heaven' (v. 13). This is the single most important title Jesus chose for himself — used over 80 times in the Gospels. In Daniel, the 'son of man' receives 'authority, glory, and sovereign power' from the Ancient of Days. The title carries both human vulnerability (son of man = mortal) and divine authority (clouds of heaven = divine transport). Jesus's self-designation holds both meanings in permanent tension.

'The saints of the Most High will receive the kingdom and will possess it forever' (v. 18). The four beasts represent empires that devour and crush — but the kingdom given to the saints endures. Daniel's apocalyptic vision reframes political reality: what looks permanent (empires) is temporary; what looks fragile (the saints) is eternal. The perspective is the message: seen from God's throne room, the power dynamics are inverted.

prayer_prompt: As Daniel sees one like a Son of Man given authority, glory, and an everlasting kingdom, consider asking God to expand your vision of Christ's reign beyond your personal life to the scope of all history.
